title: Regression: Llama 3.3 70B -2.9 pts
summary: 2 model(s) regressed week-over-week; worst was Llama 3.3 70B at -2.9 pts. Auto-logged — confirm it's real before writing it up.

body.p: Auto-logged when the scheduled probe flagged a week-over-week regression. Before this becomes a post, check the run log and the Reliability metric — a rate limit or provider outage can look exactly like a regression. If it holds up, the written explanation goes here.

title: The drift that was a rate limit
summary: Llama 3.3 70B "dropped" 66 points overnight. It was Groq's 30 RPM cap, not the model — and a rate limit scoring as 0% looks exactly like the thing this board exists to catch.

body.p: On 2026-07-20 the board showed Llama 3.3 70B fall from 69% to 3%, and Llama 3.1 8B to 0% — overnight, on a frozen suite. That is a regression post. It wasn't drift.
The run log told the real story:

body.code:
api.groq.com -> 429: Rate limit reached for model `llama-3.3-70b-versatile`
service tier `on_demand` ... requests per minute (RPM): Limit 30, Used 30

body.p: 34 of 35 calls, both Llama models, were rate-limited. They run on Groq's free tier — 30 RPM shared across the org — and probing two models back-to-back spent the budget. Every 429'd call scored as a wrong answer, so accuracy cratered toward zero. Google threw a few 503s and xAI a 429 too, same category.

The model didn't get dumber; a rate limit scored as a 0%. And a rate limit scoring as a 0% is the single most misleading thing a drift tracker can do — it looks exactly like the thing it exists to catch. Switch to the Reliability metric (button below) and you can see it: the share of calls that succeeded fell off a cliff for those two models, which is the honest signal that the probe, not the model, failed.

The board already tracked reliability separately and left the failed probes out of the auto-written summary, so it never claimed a regression. But the accuracy chart still dove toward 0, and there was no retry. So the probe was hardened: retry 429/5xx honoring Retry-After, throttle each provider to its rate cap, and drop points below a reliability floor from the accuracy line. Reliability keeps the dip — that's the true signal.
The hard part of an eval board isn't detecting drift. It's not manufacturing it. The most valuable thing the board did that week was stop a post from going out.
